questions.text: Why does the Surah begin with the contrast of night and day?
questions.answer: To illustrate that just as night and day differ, human efforts and outcomes differ too.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches everyone’s effort leads to the same result.
questions.answer: False. It teaches efforts are diverse and lead to different outcomes.

questions.text: What is one daily-life example of 'diverse effort' (shatta)?
questions.answer: Two people may work hard—one for Allah and honesty, another for ego and الدنيا—so outcomes differ.

questions.text: How does the Surah define the successful path in simple traits?
questions.answer: Giving, fearing Allah, and believing the best promise (reward and truth).

questions.text: How does the Surah define the unsuccessful path in simple traits?
questions.answer: Stinginess, self-sufficiency pride, and denial of the best promise.

questions.text: Why is 'thinking oneself free of need' spiritually dangerous?
questions.answer: It creates arrogance and makes a person stingy and resistant to guidance.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches wealth can save someone even if they deny Allah.
questions.answer: False. It teaches wealth will not benefit when a person falls into destruction.

questions.text: What does the Surah mean by being eased toward the easy path versus the difficult path?
questions.answer: Easy path: Allah facilitates the path of goodness for those who choose faith, charity, and taqwa. Difficult path: Those who choose denial and greed find themselves pushed deeper into hardship and sin.

questions.text: How does Surah Al-Layl teach that guidance ultimately belongs to Allah?
questions.answer: It states Allah owns guidance and outcomes while still holding humans accountable for choices.

questions.text: How can a believer avoid giving charity for social credit?
questions.answer: Give secretly, avoid reminding others, and make intention only for Allah’s pleasure.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches the most righteous give to repay favors.
questions.answer: False. It teaches they give without seeking repayment or thanks.

questions.text: What does ‘seeking the countenance of his Lord’ mean in charity?
questions.answer: Giving purely to please Allah, not to impress people or gain influence.

questions.text: How can the Surah guide someone who is becoming stingy due to fear of the future?
questions.answer: Remember Allah owns the Hereafter and dunya; give with trust and you will be eased.

questions.text: What is one practical habit to fight stinginess?
questions.answer: Set an automatic daily or weekly charity amount, even if small, and never skip it.

questions.text: How does Surah Al-Layl balance hope and fear?
questions.answer: It offers ease for the giver with taqwa and warns of blazing fire for the denier and miser.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that the path to Allah is vague and unclear.
questions.answer: False. It outlines clear traits for each path.

questions.text: How can someone move from the ‘difficult way’ to the ‘easy way’?
questions.answer: Repent sincerely, begin giving, build taqwa, and believe Allah’s promises.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about who is kept away from the Fire?
questions.answer: The most righteous—those who give to purify themselves for Allah alone.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah suggests good character is separate from belief.
questions.answer: False. It links belief with generosity and taqwa.

questions.text: What are the three oaths Allah takes at the beginning of Surah Al-Layl?
questions.answer: The night as it covers, the day as it appears/shines, and the creation of the male and female.

questions.text: How does Allah describe human effort in this Surah?
questions.answer: As being 'diverse' (Shatta) - varying in nature and goal.

questions.text: What three things does the 'successful' person do in this Surah?
questions.answer: Gives [in charity], fears Allah (Taqwa), and believes in the 'Best' (Al-Husna/Paradise).

questions.text: What will Allah ease for this successful person?
questions.answer: Toward the 'Easy Way' (Al-Yusra).

questions.text: What three things does the 'unsuccessful' person do in this Surah?
questions.answer: Is stingy, considers himself free of need, and denies the 'Best' (Paradise).

questions.text: What will Allah ease for this unsuccessful person?
questions.answer: Toward the 'Difficult Way' (Al-'Usra).

questions.text: Will wealth benefit a person when he falls into destruction?
questions.answer: No.

questions.text: Whose responsibility is guidance according to this Surah?
questions.answer: Indeed, upon Allah is guidance.

questions.text: To whom belongs the Hereafter and the first [life]?
questions.answer: To Allah.

questions.text: How does this Surah describe the Fire?
questions.answer: A Fire that blazes (Naran taladh-dha).

questions.text: Who will enter this blazing Fire?
questions.answer: The 'most wretched' (Al-Ashqa).

questions.text: What two traits define the 'most wretched' person in this Surah?
questions.answer: He denied the truth and turned away.

questions.text: Who will be 'kept away' from the Fire?
questions.answer: The 'most righteous' (Al-Atqa).

questions.text: What does the 'most righteous' person do with his wealth?
questions.answer: He gives his wealth to purify himself.

questions.text: Does the righteous person give charity to repay a favor?
questions.answer: No, he gives 'not for any favor to be compensated'.

questions.text: What is the only motivation for the righteous person's charity?
questions.answer: Seeking the countenance of his Lord, the Most High.

questions.text: What is the final promise for the most righteous person?
questions.answer: He will surely be satisfied/well-pleased.

questions.text: What is 'Al-Husna'?
questions.answer: It is interpreted as 'The Best Word' (Lailaha illa Allah) or the 'Best Reward' (Paradise).

questions.text: Does Allah force a person to be wretched or righteous?
questions.answer: No, He eases them toward what they have already chosen through their actions and beliefs.

questions.text: What is the link between 'Stinginess' and 'Self-Sufficiency' in this Surah?
questions.answer: Thinking one is 'free of need' (Istaghna) of Allah leads directly to stinginess and denial of the truth.
